summary.intent: Fix checkpoints not showing in the VSCode treeview, then refactor the server to use per-repo DB clients instead of a single primary DB.
summary.outcome: Checkpoints treeview was fixed (root cause was stale server running pre-schema-fix code with empty tables). Server was refactored to use a dbRegistry with allDbs(), dbForRepo(), and resolveDb() helpers. A secondary worktree creation bug was also fixed using --detach.

repo: Each repo has its own SQLite DB — there is no primary/hierarchy DB concept. The server must aggregate across all repo DBs for list endpoints.
The checkpoint worktree branch is entire/checkpoints/v1. Multiple repos can share this branch name, so worktree add must use --detach to avoid 'branch already used' conflicts.
The CheckpointTreeProvider.ts only populates when setSession() is called from a session detail panel — it is not a global tree.
The server is started via serve.ts with --repo-dir, which previously determined the single 'primary' db. This concept has been removed.
pushSchema bugs caused empty tables — stale server processes from before schema fixes can silently return empty arrays, masking data that actually exists.

code.finding: Old endpoint used bare `db` (single primary Prisma client) for checkpoint queries — replaced with resolveDb() pattern

dbRegistry (Map<dbPath, PrismaClient>) built at startup from all configured repos. allDbs() returns all clients, dbForRepo(localPath) resolves a specific repo's client, resolveDb(localPath, probe) falls back to probing all DBs.
worktree add command changed to include --detach flag to avoid branch-lock conflicts when multiple repos share the same checkpoint branch name
Simplified: no longer computes or passes dbPath to startServer. Signature is now startServer(port, repoDir).
setSession() must be called (triggered by opening a session detail panel) before checkpoints appear in the tree

workflow: When an API returns empty arrays unexpectedly, check whether the running server process is stale (pre-dating a schema migration) — restart the server before debugging the query logic.
Building both the core server and the VSCode extension after schema/server changes is necessary to confirm end-to-end correctness.
Using curl to test API endpoints directly is an effective way to confirm data is flowing before debugging the UI layer.

friction: The root cause of the treeview issue was a stale server process with empty tables, not the query logic — this was misleading and required empirical DB state checks to diagnose.
The user had to explicitly clarify there is no primary DB concept; the original code architecture assumed one, requiring a significant refactor.
The worktree creation error (branch already used) only surfaced at runtime when a second repo sharing the same branch was indexed — not caught at build time.
open_items: The /api/sessions/:id and /api/v2/sessions/:id/* endpoints require a ?localPath= query param for DB resolution — the VSCode extension may need updating to pass this param when opening session details.
The resolveDb() probe-all-DBs fallback (used when ?localPath= is absent) may be slow at scale with many repos — consider caching session-to-repo mappings.
POST /api/repos and DELETE /api/repos now maintain dbRegistry, but it's unclear if the indexer/watcher lifecycle is fully synchronized with registry add/evict on dynamic repo changes.
